research_equation.formulation: Agency = f(Goals, Identity, Decision-Making, Self-Regulation, Learning) | World-Model-Grounded

independent_variables.name: Goal Internalization
independent_variables.formalized_as: p_δ(g_t | s_t, g) — learned hierarchical decomposition module

independent_variables.name: Identity Internalization
independent_variables.formalized_as: p_ι(i_t | s_t, i_{t-1}) — identity evolver, Theorem 1

independent_variables.name: Decision-Making Grounding
independent_variables.formalized_as: p_π_f(c_t | s_t, g_t, i_t) — world-model-grounded planner, Theorem 2

independent_variables.name: Self-Regulation Depth
independent_variables.formalized_as: p_κ(u_t | s_t, g_t, i_t, c_{t-1}) — System III meta-controller

independent_variables.name: Learning Autonomy
independent_variables.formalized_as: configurator-governed interleaving of acting and learning

PEG_framework.Performance: Task success with generalizable reasoning (goal decomp, identity adapt, simulative planning)
PEG_framework.Efficiency: Compute allocation quality (latency, thinking tokens vs accuracy, planning frequency)
PEG_framework.Growth: Learning curves (learning efficiency, self-directed exploration, learning transfer)

research_equation.key_constraint: Agent model p_π(a|s,g,i) must remain functionally distinct from world model p_f(s'|s,a); collapsed models conflate reward-driven action selection with fidelity-driven prediction

tri_pass_notes.first_pass_summary: Philosophical-framework paper establishing a principled boundary between scaffolded automation (agentic) and genuine agency (agentive). Proposes GIC architecture integrating five dimensions. Two formal theorems. No empirical experiments — experimental validation deferred to companion papers.

sections.§1_Introduction: Motivates agency from Descartes, Blade Runner. Defines agentic vs agentive. Introduces five dimensions. States AM = p_π(a|s,g,i). Introduces GIC at high level.

sections.§2_Agentic_vs_Agentive: Formalizes agent-environment model. Five subsections each formalizing one dimension along a spectrum. Key equation (1): factorization of trajectory into agent and universe factors.

sections.§3_Landscape: Six-category taxonomy of current systems from program-based to embodied-model. Relation to six existing surveys. Observation: core agency structures still reside outside the model.

sections.§4_Critique: Deep critique of five common practices. Each: status quo → limitation → constructive alternative. Identifies common root cause — absence of explicit world model. Two theorems.

sections.§5_GIC_Architecture: Presents complete GIC proposal: 6 agent model components + 1 world model. Three-system cognitive architecture (I/II/III). Three-phase training. PEG evaluation. Data requirements. Safety analysis.

sections.§6_Conclusion: Not definitive answers but inspiration. GIC as principled path. Future directions: multi-agent, time scales, modalities, perpetual learning.

strengths: Principled formal framework grounded in decision theory (agent-environment factorization)
Comprehensive: covers philosophical, formal, architectural, practical, and safety dimensions
Theorems provide testable formal claims with explicit assumptions
Novel agentic vs agentive taxonomy clarifies a muddy conceptual landscape
GIC architecture is modular and addresses a gap (no existing framework integrates all five dimensions)
Safety argument (component-level auditability) is principled and addresses real concerns about opaque systems
weaknesses: No empirical validation — all experimental results deferred to companion papers
Computational cost of GIC is unanalyzed (multiple interacting components, separate world model)
Theorem assumptions (A1, A2) may be strong — 'better than random' identity revision is non-trivial
World model accuracy assumption (TV error ≤ ε) is a first-order concern for open-ended environments
No discussion of parameter scales, memory budgets, or engineering feasibility of the full architecture
Goal-oriented data is identified as crucial but its collection/scaling at variety is hand-waved
Multi-agent extension (nested world models) is mentioned but not developed
gaps: No quantitative analysis of GIC's sample complexity vs existing approaches
No discussion of how to train the configurator κ beyond 'refined via RL'
No treatment of identity representation — what form does i_t take? Neural embedding? Symbolic?
Safety argument is theoretical — no red-teaming or adversarial evaluation
No concrete benchmark or evaluation protocol for the PEG framework
Computational efficiency of the three-system architecture vs monolithic models unaddressed
critical_assumptions: World model can be trained to sufficient accuracy (bounded TV error) for open-ended environments
Identity evolver ι can learn to produce better-than-random revisions from interaction experience
Slow update operator 𝒰 is monotone in both base-policy and data-generating-policy quality
Goal-oriented data can be curated at sufficient scale and variety
Layered transparency in practice leads to better safety outcomes than monolithic approaches
The separation of agent and world model can be maintained without sacrificing end-to-end performance

challenge_module_map.root_challenge: How to build machine learning models with genuine, general-purpose agency — capable of autonomous, continuous, and self-directed operation in open-ended environments

sub_challenges.challenge: Scalable long-horizon goal pursuit without human step-by-step instruction
sub_challenges.key_idea: Isolate long-horizon difficulty in learned decomposition module; short-horizon subgoals are easier to learn and supervise
open_questions: How to train δ without dense reward signals at the subgoal level?
How to ensure δ revisits decomposition appropriately when plans fail?
How to learn δ that generalizes across diverse goal spaces?

sub_challenges.challenge: Self-model that adapts to new capabilities, environments, and constraints without retraining
sub_challenges.key_idea: Fast-slow update: identity revisions take effect immediately (fast), parameter updates consolidate durable changes (slow)
open_questions: What is the right representation for identity i_t? (embeddings? symbolic? hybrid?)
How to bootstrap ι when the agent has little interaction history?
How does identity interact with goal decomposition — can identity influence which goals are pursued?

sub_challenges.challenge: Grounded decision-making that goes beyond narrative plausibility of chain-of-thought
sub_challenges.gic_module: π_f (Simulative Planner / System II) + α (Actor / System I)
sub_challenges.gic_formulation: c_t ~ p_π_f(·|s_t, g_t, i_t); a_t ~ p_α(·|s_t, c_t)
sub_challenges.key_idea: Plan via predicting consequences through a separately trained world model f; Theorem 2 guarantees improvement if f is sufficiently accurate
open_questions: How to efficiently search action spaces under the world model? (Equation 3 is intractable)
What is the optimal trade-off between planning horizon depth and computation cost?
How does the critic evaluator v interact with the planner?

sub_challenges.challenge: Metacognitive control of when and how much to deliberate, learn, and revise
sub_challenges.key_idea: Learn a meta-controller that adaptively allocates computation (System I vs II vs III) and schedules learning
open_questions: How to train κ without a separate supervisory signal for meta-decisions?
What is the right granularity of regulation variables u_t? (binary mode switch? continuous budget?)
How to prevent the configurator from becoming a model of task difficulty that itself needs regulation?

sub_challenges.challenge: Autonomous, continuous, self-directed learning from both real and simulated experience
sub_challenges.key_idea: Learning is not a separate phase — it is governed by the configurator as part of the agent's ongoing decision-making
open_questions: How to prevent catastrophic forgetting during continuous learning?
How to decide when simulated experience is sufficient vs real interaction is necessary?
How to credit-assign across learning episodes in open-ended environments?

sub_challenges.challenge: Building a world model accurate enough for reliable planning while general enough for open-ended environments
sub_challenges.gic_module: f (World Model, separate from Agent Model)
sub_challenges.key_idea: Trained on next-state prediction alone; no agent reward signal flows into WM parameters; GLP architecture (Xing et al. 2025)
open_questions: How to handle combinatorial state spaces (open worlds)?
How to model other agents within the world model?
When should the agent update/recalibrate its world model during deployment?

sub_challenges.challenge: Ensuring safety in systems with persistent goals, evolving identity, and autonomous learning
sub_challenges.gic_formulation: Safety = audit(δ) + monitor(ι) + inspect(f) + verify(κ) + review(learning)
sub_challenges.key_idea: Modularity makes safety a component-debugging problem; all capabilities are explicit and inspectable by design
open_questions: Does modular auditability scale to the complexity of real-world deployment?
What are the failure modes when individual components are well-trained but interact unexpectedly?
How to handle cases where goal g itself is harmful (beyond scope of architectural safety)?
